topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes still hung heavy in the studio air, a familiar, acrid blanket. I stepped back from the canvas, my breath catching in my throat. There, beneath the vibrant strokes of my latest abstract piece, was a line. A precise, dark etching that wasn't mine. My hand, usually steady enough to coax life from pigment, trembled slightly as I leaned closer. It was a miniature portrait, rendered in stark black ink, depicting the very subject I had painstakingly captured in oils. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I snatched a clean cloth, my movements sharp and jerky, and scrubbed at the mark, the friction doing little to erase the memory of the intrusion. "Who," I muttered, the sound tight and low, "did this?"

[story 2]

I stared at the photograph on the easel, the one I’d just finished. It was a study of fractured light, all sharp angles and deep shadows. Then I saw it—a delicate, almost mocking script woven into the background, a pattern that mirrored the composition perfectly, yet was undeniably foreign. A sudden, cold knot tightened in my stomach. I paced the floor, my boots clicking loudly on the wooden planks. I stopped abruptly, planting my feet wide, and ran a hand through my already disheveled hair. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscle strain beneath my skin. "This is absurd," I whispered to the empty room, the tone laced with a simmering frustration that vibrated in the silence. I needed answers, and whoever had done this had clearly misjudged the audacity of the situation.

[story 3]

The gallery opening was a blur of polite chatter and forced smiles. I was examining my portfolio when my eyes snagged on a piece hanging nearby—a landscape I hadn't even seen yet. Zooming in, I realized the detail: a tiny, almost invisible tattoo tracing the contour of a distant mountain range. It was a deliberate, almost arrogant appropriation of my vision. A wave of sharp annoyance washed over me, making my shoulders tense up to my ears. I walked directly toward the painting, my stride purposeful and stiff. I didn't speak immediately; instead, I circled the piece twice, my gaze raking over the surface as if trying to physically peel away the offending mark. When I finally turned, my expression was set—a hard, unyielding line. "This is completely unacceptable," I stated, my voice cutting through the surrounding noise with an unexpected, brittle sharpness.
